illinois.h2: Illinois's veteran community and Camp Lejeune claims
illinois.body: Illinois is home to approximately 493,667 veterans (Housing Assistance Council analysis of 2019–2023 Census American Community Survey data), and its military heritage runs through installations such as Naval Station Great Lakes, the Navy's largest training center. Service members, family members, and civilian workers from Illinois who lived or worked at Marine Corps Base Camp Lejeune for at least 30 cumulative days between 1953 and 1987 are part of the group the Camp Lejeune Justice Act allows to seek compensation; those who filed in time are now pursuing those claims in federal court in North Carolina.

sources.label: Veterans Data Central (Housing Assistance Council) — Illinois veterans, 2019–2023 ACS

faqs.question: How many veterans live in Illinois, and where are their Camp Lejeune claims decided?
faqs.answer: Illinois is home to approximately 493,667 veterans (2019–2023 American Community Survey). Any Illinois veteran, family member, or civilian worker who spent at least 30 cumulative days at Camp Lejeune between 1953 and 1987 and filed a claim before the August 10, 2024 deadline has that claim decided in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of North Carolina, which holds exclusive jurisdiction under the Camp Lejeune Justice Act. No Illinois state court hears these claims, and no Illinois statute of limitations applies.

missouri.h2: Missouri's veteran community and Camp Lejeune claims
missouri.body: Missouri is home to approximately 354,505 veterans (Housing Assistance Council analysis of 2019–2023 Census American Community Survey data), with a military presence anchored by Fort Leonard Wood and Whiteman Air Force Base. Veterans, family members, and civilian workers from Missouri who lived or worked at Marine Corps Base Camp Lejeune for at least 30 cumulative days between 1953 and 1987 are part of the group the Camp Lejeune Justice Act allows to seek compensation; those who filed in time are now pursuing those claims in federal court in North Carolina.

texas.h2: Texas's veteran community and Camp Lejeune claims
texas.body: Texas has the largest veteran population of any U.S. state — approximately 1,401,252 veterans (2023 Census American Community Survey, via RAND) — anchored by installations such as Fort Cavazos (formerly Fort Hood) and Joint Base San Antonio. Service members, family members, and civilian workers from Texas who lived or worked at Marine Corps Base Camp Lejeune for at least 30 cumulative days between 1953 and 1987 are part of the group the Camp Lejeune Justice Act allows to seek compensation; those who filed in time are now pursuing those claims in federal court in North Carolina.

No Texas state court hears these claims, and no Texas statute of limitations applies.

florida.h2: Florida's veteran community and Camp Lejeune claims
florida.body: Florida has the second-largest veteran population in the nation — approximately 1,335,546 veterans (2023 Census American Community Survey, via RAND) — with major installations including MacDill Air Force Base and Naval Air Station Pensacola. Service members, family members, and civilian workers from Florida who lived or worked at Marine Corps Base Camp Lejeune for at least 30 cumulative days between 1953 and 1987 are part of the group the Camp Lejeune Justice Act allows to seek compensation; those who filed in time are now pursuing those claims in federal court in North Carolina.

california.h2: California's veteran community and Camp Lejeune claims
california.body: California has the third-largest veteran population in the nation — approximately 1,243,018 veterans (2023 Census American Community Survey, via RAND) — and is home to Marine Corps Base Camp Pendleton, the Corps' largest West Coast installation. Service members, family members, and civilian workers from California who lived or worked at Marine Corps Base Camp Lejeune for at least 30 cumulative days between 1953 and 1987 are part of the group the Camp Lejeune Justice Act allows to seek compensation; those who filed in time are now pursuing those claims in federal court in North Carolina.

Any California veteran, family member, or civilian worker who spent at least 30 cumulative days at Camp Lejeune between 1953 and 1987 and filed a claim before the August 10, 2024 deadline has that claim decided in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of North Carolina, which holds exclusive jurisdiction under the Camp Lejeune Justice Act. No California state court hears these claims, and no California statute of limitations applies.

north-carolina.h2: North Carolina's veteran community and Camp Lejeune claims
north-carolina.body: North Carolina is home to just over 615,440 veterans (2023 Census American Community Survey, via UNC's Carolina Demography) — a higher share of its adult population than the national average — and to Marine Corps Base Camp Lejeune itself, in Jacksonville, along with Fort Liberty and Marine Corps Air Station Cherry Point. North Carolina residents who lived or worked at Camp Lejeune for at least 30 cumulative days between 1953 and 1987 are part of the group the Camp Lejeune Justice Act allows to seek compensation, and their claims are heard close to home, in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of North Carolina — the federal court the Act gives exclusive jurisdiction.

sources.label: UNC Carolina Demography — North Carolina 2023 Veterans Snapshot

faqs.question: How many veterans live in North Carolina, and where are their Camp Lejeune claims decided?
faqs.answer: North Carolina is home to just over 615,440 veterans (2023 American Community Survey) and to Camp Lejeune itself. Any North Carolina veteran, family member, or civilian worker who spent at least 30 cumulative days at the base between 1953 and 1987 and filed a claim before the August 10, 2024 deadline has that claim decided in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of North Carolina, which holds exclusive jurisdiction under the Camp Lejeune Justice Act. No state statute of limitations applies.

georgia.h2: Georgia's veteran community and Camp Lejeune claims
georgia.body: Georgia is home to approximately 608,876 veterans (Housing Assistance Council analysis of 2019–2023 Census American Community Survey data), with a military presence anchored by Fort Moore (formerly Fort Benning), Fort Stewart, and Moody Air Force Base. Service members, family members, and civilian workers from Georgia who lived or worked at Marine Corps Base Camp Lejeune for at least 30 cumulative days between 1953 and 1987 are part of the group the Camp Lejeune Justice Act allows to seek compensation; those who filed in time are now pursuing those claims in federal court in North Carolina.

ohio.h2: Ohio's veteran community and Camp Lejeune claims
ohio.body: Ohio had approximately 681,106 veterans as of September 30, 2023 (U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs FY2023 data, via Spectrum News), with Wright-Patterson Air Force Base among its largest installations. Veterans, family members, and civilian workers from Ohio who lived or worked at Marine Corps Base Camp Lejeune for at least 30 cumulative days between 1953 and 1987 are part of the group the Camp Lejeune Justice Act allows to seek compensation; those who filed in time are now pursuing those claims in federal court in North Carolina.

pennsylvania.h2: Pennsylvania's veteran community and Camp Lejeune claims
pennsylvania.body: Pennsylvania is home to approximately 619,472 veterans (2023 Census American Community Survey, via RAND), with a military presence that includes Carlisle Barracks, home of the U.S. Army War College. Service members, family members, and civilian workers from Pennsylvania who lived or worked at Marine Corps Base Camp Lejeune for at least 30 cumulative days between 1953 and 1987 are part of the group the Camp Lejeune Justice Act allows to seek compensation; those who filed in time are now pursuing those claims in federal court in North Carolina.

michigan.h2: Michigan's veteran community and Camp Lejeune claims
michigan.body: Michigan is home to more than 441,000 veterans — about 5.6% of the state's population (2023 Census American Community Survey, via the Michigan Center for Data and Analytics) — with Selfridge Air National Guard Base among its primary installations. Veterans, family members, and civilian workers from Michigan who lived or worked at Marine Corps Base Camp Lejeune for at least 30 cumulative days between 1953 and 1987 are part of the group the Camp Lejeune Justice Act allows to seek compensation; those who filed in time are now pursuing those claims in federal court in North Carolina.
